speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Edna Johnson Butterfield was born in 1890 at Woodfell, a community north of Harvard. She lived there until age 20 when she married Orville Butterfield and moved to two miles west of Harvard. On this tape, Edna Butterfield describes her parents' arrival in the United States and their move to Woodville. She talks of her father packing groceries up to the Hutu Miners and of her house, which was a halfway house for travelers from Harvard to Emida. She also mentions school, entertainment, the 1893 Depression, and doctoring.

words: When did you go to school?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: In the summertime. Mostly. They'd be a college teacher. Or someone that was going to college would come and teach. And at that time they were paying the teachers $35 a month. And the teachers would have to pay $10 a month for boards. So you see what they made then and what they're making now. Yeah, see what they can live on now, too. Or could live on then. Someone would board me for $10 a day. I'd be impressed. Well, that's what they did.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did they board with you? Who did they board with?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, my mother board several of them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were most of them pretty young, the teachers?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Most of them, yes.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Most of them were girls that were just around somebody to go to school with and fall.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was Harvard the nearest real town?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Harvard was the nearest town to... Well, Harvard wasn't there then.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When did Harvard start growing as a town?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Harvard started growing. They started growing. It was about...

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: 1900 and... It must have been about 1900 and eight or nine. When that was the Harvard start. So Harvard didn't start growing until they put the railroad in then. No. No, there was no town at Harvard. Actually, there is no community there. But Princeton, there were... Well, Princeton was an older town. And years ago, it was called Starnier. And there was an old fellow by the name of Starnier that had the post office.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He had the mail and things there. And then afterwards, there was somebody else coming in. But he left and there was another party coming in there.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How come they changed the name of the town from Starnier?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, I don't know how it came because the man that started it, his name was Starnier. And then when they changed it, they changed it to Princeton. And then when Harvard started, they started... They were having college towns. There was Princeton and Harvard. And then there was a little town up there. Up the railroad. There was Yale.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: There used to be a Stanford up there, don't there?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, a Stanford. There was a lot of them little towns. And that was in the little later years.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why they got it into their heads to name everything after colleges?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, and there was Dury. There was a man by the name of Dury. He came in there and he located the place called Dury. And then he was the one that had these little towns all named after college towns. He didn't change.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Princeton didn't get its name that way, though, right?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, Princeton didn't that way. But Harvard did.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Did you go...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever remember going into Palouse?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you go there very much?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Maybe once every five or six years. Well, my folks had to drive up to the team and we just didn't get to go. Once in a while, my sister and I would go with my father down in Palouse. I had an aunt back down there. We'd go down there and see if we'd done any shopping and then we'd come back home again.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember of Palouse?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Palouse was a bigger town than it is now. Palouse was a better town than it is now.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was it a pretty bustling place? Was it a pretty bustling place? Were there a lot of people around?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, there was more then. There were lots of saloons. At one time, I can remember, in Princeton there were seven saloons.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And there was lots of saloons and flutes, too, in those places. And there was so much drinking going on in those days.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: There's drinking enough now, but I don't know. It seemed as if there was so much going on then.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, it sounds like, from what I've heard, that it was really pretty bad back then, the drinking.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: You mentioned that your parents sold butter and cheese.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where did they sell that to?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They'd just pack it up in the hoodoo to the new fellas that was living up there. You see, they'd mine and make a little bowl to buy their groceries. And he'd take his butter and eggs and cheese from whatever they had up there and so on. And then they'd send with him for groceries, too, out to the police station and other places.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were your parents ever able to leave home together?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, they never thought of it. They were paying them leave.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: One of them had to be there. These people can go somewhere and the whole family can go, it don't matter. But those days, they just thought they couldn't.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what kind of livestock you had around the place? About how much?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, we had horses and cattle and sheep.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: About how much of each would you have? How much what? How much of each would you usually have?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, they never kept more than 10 or 12 head of cattle. And they'd keep what horses they need to work with. Oh, maybe four or five. And then they'd keep maybe 10 or 15 sheep. And they'd have the sheep they'd do for mutton and sell the wool off them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother card the wool or anything?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: My grandmother did. But my mother didn't. But my grandmother did.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: She didn't live with you, did she? She what? She didn't live with you, did she?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes. Oh.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: She came out with you from, with your parents, rather?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, she came after they did. But she came out to my parents and she carted wool and made yarn and stockings.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That's something no one does anymore.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the girls mainly work indoors and the boys work outdoors with the livestock?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, yes, I think they did. More so than they do now. The women work outdoors more than they did at that time.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You didn't help on any kind of harvests or anything very much then? Didn't help us? On the harvest or?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, no. And we didn't have much harvest. At that time there was no combine tramp. We was little, they'd cut some hay for the cattle and horses for winter. And that was all. They didn't have any combines. Nobody knew what was up in there to thrash with.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Is that mainly all you grew was hay?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That's right.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Hay?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was it the wild Timothy?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: A lot of it was. Until he finally got some plowed up and was a little more tame Timothy. But it was mostly wild Timothy.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have to reseed that, did you?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Not so much.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How would they harvest the Timothy? How would they harvest it? Would they just?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, they didn't want to rake it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: And then would they just get it in big piles and leave it?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, they would kind of dry it and then stack it there and put it in the barn. But it never happened to have.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did they like rake the, or fork the stacks of hay into wagons and then take them to the barn? Is that how they?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, uh-huh.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Would you get help from the neighbors for doing that?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh no, they never had enough of what they could do their own. They didn't do a lot of harvesting and things like they do now.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to visit with other families very much?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, they visited more than they do today. They'd go from one house to the other and they'd have what they call these little party dances. Mostly on Friday or Saturday night a bunch of them would go to a house and play and dance. And there's a few musicians that would play.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Mostly fiddlers?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You came along even when you were a little kid? Did I? You went along even when you were pretty small?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, I wasn't very large. When I went with my father I went with the literaries. I couldn't have been more than five or six years old when I went with the end of the literaries. And when he used to go, well he did quite a little debating at the literaries like they did in those days. And he took some of us older children with him when he'd go.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where would they be held? Where would they have them?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They'd have them in the schoolhouses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: They'd have them in a schoolhouse pretty near you then?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, in a car.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any of the topics that they used to debate?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I don't. You don't remember any of them.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I wouldn't have any idea.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I just don't remember any of the subjects they debated on. I know my second oldest brother, he sure liked to debate and do those things. But of course he's gone now. He's older than I was.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Would they announce it ahead of time and then people would... Well, they might, Friday night ahead of time or something. It was just the neighbors that would do it. And they'd select teams to see which one could do the best debating and see who'd do the best. And then there'd be some judges to tell which one was the best.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So it wouldn't just be two individuals that would speak on either side?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You mean like there'd be eight people and four people would speak for one position and four for another and then they'd judge which side won?

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How long would that last into the night? Would that last very long?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, boy. Sometimes ten o 'clock or so.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I heard that when they used to celebrate Christmas around here that they didn't give gifts, that that wasn't part of Christmas. No, but the way they were celebrated Christmas is at the schools they'd have a Christmas program and have treats for the children. And, you know, the Christmas program got up by the children and then... And they always really enjoyed that. Even the grown-ups enjoyed the children's programs and getting up those things for them. But you'd have skits and you'd have plays and... Yes, uh-huh.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to celebrate Thanksgiving?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, yes, I do, yes. I like that one. We had forty-five here for dinner last Thanksgiving. No, I have quite a few relatives and we generally, they generally get together here at Thanksgiving time. More than at Christmas time.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Are there any other holidays that you remember that you'd celebrate?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, the Fourth of July they'd have, they generally, some place they'd have a celebration and they had some old places that they used to have dances in the Fourth of July. That was about the only thing.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Did you ever see, were there ever circuses or...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Circuses?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Circuses. Oh, well, I didn't hear a circus that we ever went to.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Was there ever been a circus in the cloudlights? We went to some in Moscow when we was kids, but I don't know how about you, Mother?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, I guess we used to, there used to be a circus in Moscow once in a while and I know they had one on the bridge. Well, I guess she wanted to go further back than what I can remember. We used to have a circus in the flutes once in a while. Of course, that was a wonderful thing for us kids that up in the woods that hadn't seen anything like that.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any medicine shows?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I don't remember any medicine shows.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I don't remember any medicine shows.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were there many families that lived around Woodfell? Near Woodfell?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No. Many families lived where?

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: That lived near Woodfell?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, it was about a mile from that nearest one. And there wasn't so many. There wasn't more than four or five in that neighborhood. And the nearest one I know was at Easter Mile. Because Woodfell wasn't started until these other towns were getting, until it had been going quite a while.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the names of any of the families that lived near you?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, there was one by the name of Mott, and there was one by the name of Draper, and Beeson, and Marthon,

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: and...

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It seems needed some knowledge, but...

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They didn't know they lived within maybe five or ten miles of us. And there was a Kinman, and Kinman, and Smith, and... But I think near every one of them were gone.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What did most of them do for a living? Did they do anything different than your parents did?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, to start with, that's about all we could do until they got... Oh, time went by and they got more land cleared, and got so they could raise more stuff. And lots of them cut wood just for living. Cut and sold wood.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Could you get by year-round just doing that, selling wood, cord wood?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, they could, yes. Because there was always a road. It was sometimes pretty muddy, but there was always a road up through where anyone could get out if they had to. And in those days, the nearest doctor was 30 miles.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What did people generally do when they got sick?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, if they got well, they did. If it was children, a good many times they died. I know one family that had a boy, and he had a lump on his side, and they didn't know what it was. They couldn't afford the doctor, and it was... he died, it was sure later on in years that he died of appendicitis.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: But my brother, when he was little, he had pneumonia, and my older brother had to ride 30 miles to get some medicine for him. He would ride horseback. So, it was really with the children, if the parents couldn't doctor him at home, why, they survived the best they could, and there were a few that... not very many that died, but there...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What were the common illnesses that were serious? The what?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: The illnesses that were really serious, especially for children. Well, of course, it would be diphtheria and scarlet fever, and measles, and hope and cough. Hope and cough was the main one. But you...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: in this area, you had all four of them?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I mean, people... Oh yes, they had all four of them. And now, when I was 11 years old, I had smallpox. That went through, and I had that when I was 11 years old. I can remember that.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did they treat them? Do you know? How did they treat you for that? How did they treat you?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, they just took care of you, and did the best they could, and then you got over it. Of course, you know, if they have proper care with that smallpox, they'll gradually get over it. And, yes, there was my father, and two of my brothers, and my sister and I, my two sisters and I, all had smallpox. They just took care of us at home. My mother, years before, had been vaccinated, so she didn't take it. She didn't get vaccinated before she came here.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Do you know... oh, you were 11.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did a lot of people get smallpox that year?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Sounds like. No. There was a man came to my father's place and stayed all night. And his face was all broke out, and my father kept looking at him. The fellow said to my father, he says, you must think I got some disease the way you're looking at me. He said, well, I didn't know what you had. He says, you looked a little bit like chicken pox or smallpox. Oh, no, I haven't got that, he said. But just in two or three weeks, my younger sister took it. So we knew he had it. And it went through the family, but there was no fatalities from it. They all recovered.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And, truth, that was not a thing that... mothers had to be prepared for and doctor the best they could. They took their own recovery, too, the whole remedies.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did they treat it?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, some of them treated it with honey and... and just some other things that way. And what they call Ohio Syrup and EpiCAD.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What they call what? What kind of syrup?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: EpiCAD. If you give them too much of that, that would make them vomit and then they'd throw up that flan and stuff.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: But that isn't what it is. I mean, I have always felt so...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any other things that happened?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I don't think of any right now, I don't believe.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: My younger sister died when she was 14, but then she had kidney trouble. We had a doctor for her.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I can't think of anything else now.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What did you use to eat usually? You know, for breakfast? What? What did you use to eat usually for breakfast? Eating?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah. Well, a lot of them liked to... some of them would grind the feet and make whole feet, have whole feet, bread and stuff like that. And some would make back feet pancakes and just whatever. Things like that.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you use to can a lot?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes. Whenever we could get a can. But we had to...

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, he played. He played everything. I don't know, he played more than Jus Harp, but he played...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: what do you call them?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Mouth Harp too.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, they were musicians, some of them andersons. But these up here, they weren't musicians. Yeah, Arthur used to play a violin a little bit.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Arthur Anderson.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That's his brother, isn't it? Isn't that the one you're talking about?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah. I think he was the youngest one. He was...

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: What did you use to eat?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: You know, for breakfast? What did you use to eat usually? You know, for breakfast? What did you use to eat usually?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, he was the youngest one. He was a professional. He was really a musician. But he got the most out of it.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Did he get killed or did he just die? He got killed, didn't he?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He got killed at automobile. That's right.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He studied music. He went to college, took music in college. He was really good.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: We were talking about the families being big.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Is that true of most of the families around when you were a kid? They were big?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They were big families. There were several big families. 8.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: There were seven. That's three. And Arthur. That's four. And Alice. Dorothy. Jenny. And Emil. They were nine.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They were nine. Yeah, they were nine. They were nine.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And how many were the Helenes?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, that I don't know. There were another big bunch.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I didn't know the Helenes. You were about that many too. So there must have been lots of kids running around all at the same time. But the Oceans, they were twelve.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They were eight boys and four girls.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: What Oceans is that?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Gustaf. Oh, Gustaf Owens. Yeah, I didn't know them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid, did you have much work you were expected to do?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, yeah. I don't know enough.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Too much.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: We were working right along.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: A little bit.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: We wasn't drove any.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Our folks didn't drive us too hard.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: But we worked right along.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: We saw it.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, he didn't. Ollie was the one that saw it. Ollie and I, we used to cross cut saw. We were the power saws.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were you still pretty young when you first used to cross cut?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, yeah. I started that when I was able to pull it. We started that when we were kids. Just little colors.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, I even saw it up here at Camp 8 in 1910 at Bovill. The public saw it on a landing there. Boy, it was hot too. We had to go.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: The bigger fellas, they played out.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: We had to drag them out to the shade. Get off the hunt on them, some of them landings. They pulled them tree lengths in. Whole tree lengths. Donkeys. Then we were supposed to saw them on the landing. All them off in logs. And they wasn't double length logs either.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: This was a picture from when I first saw Moscow, already a big town. Bill was 10, I was 7, and that little squirt was on the tree. That's the family picture. We had to go to Moscow town.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That's quite a nice picture. Oh, the picture is our mine.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: This brother is 99 years old. This one died when he was 74.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Your father has a very handsome beard.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: A full beard. Yes, I never saw him without a beard. I don't think any of us did. No, I don't remember. He had a beard all the time. I have a picture of him without a beard when he was young. But that's the only time I've seen him without a beard.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He was born with a bearded man.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I got a picture of him when he doesn't have a beard.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He was born in 1845. I know.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That picture is when he was young. It's before he was married.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I bet you've never seen that picture. No, I never saw that picture.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It's a wonderful picture. It had been abused.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Our house burned up and all the pictures burned up. This was given to me afterwards by a friend of mine. It's been kind of abused.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, yeah, that's the old family picture.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: You said you were telling me a little bit that a lot of people just about walked away from the places when they first came out. Oh, yeah, there were several of them that walked away from the place. They come in here in Homestead and they couldn't see it anyway to make it here. There were several of them that went out. They built a cabin and lived probably a while in it. They got mad, I guess, and went. I tell you, they had to be tough. They couldn't figure on they were going to make it. They'd have to figure they had to make it. They didn't have no other place to go and to stay. They were tough. There was no way around it. Some of them worked to keep their family on the farm. This Anderson up here, he worked down at Genesee, walked home every Saturday and back every Saturday afternoon, from the afternoon. He worked on that all summer long. You got that in the book, in Arthur Burke's book. That was the Anderson. I know that was right too. Six bits a day.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: August Anderson?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, it was John Anderson. He was a living neighbor to Arthur Burke up here. It's in Arthur Burke's book about that fellow. Him and his wife, they put that 75 cents a day away and they never found it. That's no doubt so, but they used it up. I know that. She was there all week. She had to have some. They used that up. It wasn't that they lost it. Anderson, he didn't lose any money. He didn't spend any either. Only for necessities, he didn't spend it just foolishly. No, I tell you, they had to be hardy guys to stay.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They had to figure on living off the land. They did too.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What about your father when he came? Was he really determined to make a go of it right here?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, sure. You best determine his right. Didn't have no other place to go. There was no chance. He left the old country because there was no chance there. He left Minnesota. He just thought he was going to better himself. I don't know whether he did or not. I guess he done just as well. He couldn't make it there in Minnesota off and on 40 acres and try to pay for it with nothing. There wasn't any better there. They broke up so they had a little wheat. That took several years before that and not anything.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They didn't get along any faster there than the ones that were in Minnesota than they did here. There was no markets. Nobody was buying anything. Nobody had any money. They didn't have any money in Minnesota anymore than they did here. They probably got to farming it a little more and got a little more money. I think the people here had a better chance to eat than they did in Minnesota because there was a lot of wild game around here at that time. Here they had... Here they had fuel anyway. They had wood. He wasn't in woods in Minnesota.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: He was on prairie?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, some brush. That was all prairie. Minnesota, there was some brush. That was all of us. Some places in Minnesota there was timber but not where they were. There wasn't any timber. So maybe that's one reason he liked the sound of this better if he was a timberman. That's the reason they came out here because they were raised in timber country in Sweden. So that's the timber they come for. You heard of this... Pierre Hockesen. He was describing this country for them. They liked it and figured they would. He was here. Helstrom was here. Then they figured they'd come out and try it too. There are people that your father had known way back in Sweden then. These... Hockesen, he'd known him back in Sweden. They were acquainted in Sweden. They were from the same part of Sweden.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: But there, where they were, there was no chance there. There was really nothing but rock. There was no ground to speak of. There might have been some lowland where they cut a little grass. There wasn't any next to lake. It was lakes, water, and rock.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: In Sweden.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: The way they described it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever say the name of the town or the county that he grew up near in Sweden?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, yeah. Well, it was Värmland. That was the same as the county.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Or would that be the same as state?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I think maybe it's in between a county and a state. Well, it was, they laid out something like counties and states. Now let's see. Värmland was, well it would be a state. And Riksdag, that would be a county. And that's where we come from, Riksdag, Sweden.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: So it was laid out just about like it is here. There wasn't much difference. I don't know what, I suppose the difference in size because the country was smaller. Well, it wouldn't be a state because it's in Sweden. Sweden is the state.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It must be a county, the same as a county.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He was talking about Värmland, wasn't he?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Värmland, yeah.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I would say that it would be a county. Of course, I don't know if the division in Sweden.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Somebody told me that it was kind of in between a county and a state because it was pretty good size, I think.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, I really don't know so I shouldn't.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, when he started here, was there a house built on the place already that Asplund had lived in or did he have to build the place too? You mean my dad?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, he had to build. There was nothing but just a little bit of a cabin and that was a very small one too. That was all there was here, just a one room cabin. So he built his own cabin. He was quite an axeman so he slabbed his. The other cabin that was built, it was built out of round logs. They used it for a chicken house afterwards.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And then he built a pretty good cabin. He slabbed the sides, both in and out, both sides, and drawed it together with a draw iron. They'd groove one side and drop the other one down in and put moss in that groove and then drop the other one in and it was plumb tight. That's the way he built. All the better built cabins were built that way.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: They called that a draw iron?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: That's called a draw iron?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, that's what they marked it with. They grooved the lower edge. And then they put moss in that, it was something like a trough. They laid a log on in that and that was plumb tight. They were warm, them cabins, they didn't get cold in them. They had any kind of heat.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So how big was the cabin that he built?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, it was quite a big cabin. I don't know what it would have been. About a 14 by 16 I suppose or 16 by 16 or something like that. And then of course they lived there the first winter and then the next summer they probably built a little more. I don't know how soon he built, but you see Dahlberg, he lived down here on the Weatherford's place. Down here on the creek. And the next year he was here, he was one of them, the promoters I call them. He bought a sawmill and he started lumbering. Then of course we'd haul logs down there and trade them for lumber. Now there was a trader at Dahlberg. There was one that traded. He'd trade for anything. It didn't make any difference whether it was or whether he had any use for it or not, but he'd trade for it. And then somebody wanted to buy, he'd sell it. Sometimes he'd sell it for money and sometimes he'd trade it to the next feller. And there wasn't anything that he wouldn't trade for. Horses or cattle or lumber or logs or anything. He was really the one that helped this country up.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Why did that help the country? Why did his trading help the country?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, they got from him and he got from them. It helped everybody.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Sounds like it.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It helped everybody. If they wanted a horse they'd go down here and trade him something for a horse. And if they wanted something there, if they wanted a cow, they'd go down there and trade him something for a cow. And that just kept around, going around. And everybody was down there trading with him. Didn't make any difference what they wanted. They'd go and ask Pete for it. His name was Pete Dahlberg. He was known the world over, I think, for Pete.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And that's a fact. He was the one that helped this country up. If it hadn't been for him, I don't think, I don't believe how they'd make it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think the trading was good because it circulated the goods that people had?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They traded between themselves. That's all they had was the circulation of the goods that they had. Now, my mother, she made butter. She was a butter woman. She made butter. After they got a hold of a cow, well, they got a cow in the first winter. They were here. And after that, of course, they raised cattle. So they had six or seven cows. Five and six and seven cows all the time. And you see, they were just turned out. Cattle was there. There was no herd law or anything. They just turned out. And then the kids went and got them in the evening and drove them home. The cows got used to that. How many times I went out for miles to get the cattle. I'd have to stand on a hill someplace and listen for a long, long time. And when I heard the bell, then I went out there and got the cattle. And I sometimes had to go a long ways too. Just a little bit of a kid. You had to do that every evening. Every evening. Putting near every evening. Sometimes they'd be closer so I wouldn't have to go so far. But otherwise, we'd have to go and round them up and drive them in every evening into the corral and milk them. And that was the work we had to do. And that was after school. You couldn't figure on anything else. You had to go. That was my job. To go and get the cattle. And I guess that was everybody's kid's job.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was it tough to get them cattle to come back?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Or was it easy to herd them back in once you...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was it tough to get the cattle herded back into the corral once you found them?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, no. The cattle knew just exactly what you wanted. All you had to come and take and howler at them. And they'd all bunch up and go in. That wasn't any effort at all. It's just the effort was to go and get them.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And find them.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And of course they had a bell, you know. And you got used to that. You could hear that bell for miles. I used to stand clear up on the hill there and hear the bell clear up on Joe Fife's meadow. And that was about... Well, it must have been a long mile anyway.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: On whose meadow?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, it's Joe Fives, they call it. There was a fellow by the name of Joe Fife. He had a place up here at Avon or below Avon there. And I used to stand over there on the Bear Creek Hill above Herman Taylor's on a high point and listen. I could hear well then, but I can't now. And I'd stand there for a long, long time. And sometimes in the evening you could hear the bell for a long, long ways. And other times you couldn't. Then you'd have to go where you thought they'd be and walk around until you heard it. You heard that bell. And then you'd go down there and you'd holler at them at the cattle. And they'd bunch right up and come on and walk right ahead of me home. They always had a cow trail, you know. Well, you're saying that your mother made butter. Yes, she made butter and she made lots of it. And she packed it in five gallon jugs, jars. And then she'd sell it, but her butter was made so it didn't spoil everybody. She didn't have any trouble to sell her butter. Some of the women had trouble to sell their butter, but she never did. She made good butter. But she really worked it too.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: She really worked all the butter. That's the secret about having good butter to work the buttermilk out of it. And she really did that. She worked the buttermilk clear out of it so that it wasn't any in there. And then it kept.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was that a really big job to get the buttermilk out?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yes, that was a big job. That was a big job. You had to, you had to, a wooden spoon. I don't know about some of them yet. I believe we would have on the farm. A wooden spoon. She'd keep working that and work all the butter out of it. Or I mean, butter milk out of it. Yeah, and then it'd take half hour to churn it. She'd have to churn it first. Oh yeah, she'd have to churn it first. And then she worked the buttermilk out of it. Oh, that was an awful job. But then she was a tough old woman.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How often would she make butter?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, I really don't know.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: What was it, once a week? Remember?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Once a week.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: You remember how often she'd use it?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Once a week.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Once a week?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I guess.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where'd she sell it? Where did she sell it?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: At the stores. She traded at the stores. Didn't sell it. There was no sale. She traded it. She traded it for groceries. But the butter was cheap and so was the groceries.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I think it was one time, I remember, we got fourteen pounds of arabica coffee. Arabica and Lion's Coffee. They were about the same price. Fourteen pounds for a dollar.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I don't know.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: What was butter selling for? Keely, you remember? You remember what butter was selling for?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: About thirty cents a pound.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Thirty cents a pound?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: The only coffee I remember... Oh, it was cheaper than that. No, he's asking how much the butter was selling for.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, about twenty-five cents or something like that. Not anymore.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Twenty-five cents?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: A pound. Yeah, that's about it. Twenty-five, I believe it was cheaper than that at times, too.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: But it was just a continuous trade. It was just a continuous round.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, Pete Dahlberg, did he trade lumber a lot? Did he get lumber for goods?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Sure. He traded lumber for logs. And then you got so much, he paid you so much lumber, so much for logs in lumber. And you took the lumber and you sold him the logs. And that was a trade.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How much bartering went on in trading like that? Did they barter very much back and forth? Do they what? Did they barter very much? Did they argue about how much a thing was worth?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No. No, there was no... everything was achieved. They didn't bargain at all. They didn't argue about it either.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, it's...

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: it is what will you take and what will you give it. And if they didn't agree, well, they'd go to the next fella.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That's the way they got along till after the Potlatch started here, about 1912 and 15.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did Dahlberg do well? Did he prosper?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, sure he did. He would have been rich, but he drank so much that he was a fella that didn't keep no money. He just drank and he just lived it up. Otherwise, he would have been a rich man. If he'd been like some of them, he'd have been well to do, awfully well to do. But he was a trader, not only in one thing, but in everything. He traded his money too when he got it, if you get the... you got a name. Yeah, there was a fella from Genesee, come up with six horses. He had six horses and four colts. He came up to buy some lumber. And he didn't have no money. He wanted to trade them colts for lumber, sure, Dahlberg traded. Then he sold the colts, he sold them around to everybody around here. Lynn got one. And, what did Lynn get?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Two, I don't remember. Got one, I know. He had it a long, long time, too. And, well, there were four people gottin' horses, that's all it took. He sold them all, he didn't have them very long. I don't know if he got any money out of it or if he traded them again, I don't know. It just went right along with him. He was out there with the road trading all the time. We got two or three horses from him, Dad. But we traded, he traded for them and we traded logs for them, where my Dad traded logs for them.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And now we bought two. Two is not more, oh, we bought more than that. Three or four horses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was he, when you traded with him, did you do well or was he more likely to get the best of you?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, no, no. He didn't try to get the best of you. That is, he didn't, with his neighbors anyway. He was a neighborly guy, I tell you. He just wanted to help his neighbors. And I think that applied to everybody that he dealt with. I think he just tried to help them.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And he was pretty well liked too, so they all tried to do the right thing with him. Well, you talk about going to the, about buying groceries at the store, you know, trading the butter.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What, besides coffee, did your family buy? Did they buy very much? I mean, did they get very much from the store?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, yeah, we bought coffee and all the sugar and everything we needed from here.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And the fish and herring and everything.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, we didn't.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you have your church services, did you have the church services in the schoolhouse?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, yeah, no, we didn't. The school didn't. But then the preachers, other preachers come in. They had church services. That was put in here every Sunday, in Sunday school. And then meeting after that, service. I went to Sunday school and I was twenty-five years old. That's the only place we had to go on Sunday was Sunday school. And I went to Sunday school and I was twenty-five years old.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That's where I learned to read Sweden.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And what little American I learned, I learned something in English school, of course, that is the week, public school.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: So I went to school all the time, that is not more on Sundays, more steady on Sundays. That was every Sunday I went to Sunday school.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the Sunday school like? Besides, you studied Swedish, right? Besides that, what did you do in Sunday school? Did you learn scripture?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, well, we learned scripture too.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Sure.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I knew the Bible by heart for a long time. There wasn't a word in the Bible that I hadn't heard and knew it. But I've forgotten now more than I, since I quit.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I went to Sunday school until I was twenty-five years old. Every Sunday, too, pretty near every Sunday. There was nothing else to go to. You stayed home all week and worked. If you didn't go to school, you stayed home and worked. When Sunday came, you had to do something, so you went to Sunday school. That was the only thing there was until Dieria started. Then they had some ball games up here. But I didn't go up there to them because there was just a... By that time, I was working. I was following horses and driving. I didn't take the horses out on Sundays much. Once in a while, I'd take one. I bought me a buggy and used to sing one horse and drove around a little bit. But we didn't run around very much.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It wasn't as handy then as it is now to jump into a car and go to... took me along.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Sunday school, was that much of a chance for socializing for the young people or was it more serious? Which?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Sunday school. Well, that was more serious. That wasn't so much socializing. Although we met, you know, we got there. It's just like a meeting. You go and then you visit after the meeting. Religious meetings. Now the Seventh Day Advent, they don't do that. They don't visit after. But there's mission. They call it the mission church. They were always visiting after the meeting and visiting with each other too.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Would there be refreshments after the meeting or just people stand around and talk?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They'd just stand around. Well, the old people, they'd go but the kids would stand there. The young people, they'd stand around a while. An hour or so.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It'd be between twelve and one when we got back from the Sunday school. Sunday dinner, sometimes two. I don't know. It all depends on how much visiting there was.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: About when did the meeting start? About what time did the service start?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh, about ten thirty or eleven or something like that. They didn't keep on very long. They probably were there about an hour.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where did the pastor come from?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They didn't have any pastor. It was one of the farmers. Asplen was one of them. Old Man Asplen and Old Man Holger. That was Archie Johnson's granddad. He was his mother's father. And then it was always Johnson was a teacher, a Sunday school teacher too. And then it was Carl Olson. Big Carl they called him. He came from out there on Dry Creek. He'd be teaching once in a while too. He was a Sunday school teacher but then he wasn't in our country or too much. He'd come down once in a while.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Are you saying that the same guys that taught Sunday school lead the church service?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were the same guys that taught Sunday school lead the church service?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, they helped. They were church members.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They were traveling ministers though that came through. They had the services most of the time.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean a lot of Sundays or most Sundays there would be a minister there?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, because they... Sometimes there was. They would be at the meetings there would be a minister but not at the Sunday school so much because them fellas did hold their Sunday school before the church services. And then the church service would take up the bus.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you had to minister at the service would he be the same one every week or would it be different ones?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Different ones too. Oh, sometimes they would come back.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They were traveling through and it depends on how long they would stay there on and so on and so forth.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, when you say they traveled... That's in the schoolhouse but the church had regular ministers hired. Yeah, well they didn't have for Sunday school regular ministers all the time. No, they were just traveling. Those ministers were traveling through. Down here at the Norah Church that was Alfred Johnson. The churches had regular ministers. I don't know, did you know them?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, I know who they were though. Yeah, they were the Sunday school teachers. But then if there was happened to be a minister around there he'd help them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were the schoolhouses used for entertainment too? Well, what do you mean?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, like I mean get-togethers without there being no church. We used to have literaries. Oh yeah, they had literaries. Sure. Yeah, that's right. They did have literaries.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what those literaries were? What they did there? The literaries?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah. No, I don't think. They were discussing, drinking, and fighting.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: It all depends on how wild the crowd was.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: And then I don't know what discussions there were. I didn't go to the literary too much. I was too young for that.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What about dances or parties?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, they didn't have that in the schoolhouses.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: They, uh,

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: no they didn't have that in the schoolhouses. Because they had benches, you know, that had to be moved. And they were tightened right to the floors. So they didn't have dances in the schoolhouses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were there parties at people's homes? Were there parties at people's houses?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, well yes, there was house parties in lots of places. We used to have house parties down around there where we lived. They danced there.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: We danced at the house parties. There was a lot of them. They were around.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That was later, that was later in the later years. When we were kids, then it was Sunday school. In the later years, then we had house parties. Birthday house parties.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Birthday?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, birthday dance. That was a certain bunch, mostly.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: That wasn't so big, that wasn't a very big crowd.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Six or seven couples or something like that. In that book, they lived very much the way we did. Just an awful lot.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Where did you get that from?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: From the head. Yeah, we did that.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Are you Ed Swenson?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: No, no, but I talked to Ed Swenson.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Big Anderson?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Oh yes, yes, I knew Big Anderson since I was a little kid.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He was here, he was here, and then he went to Canada. And then he came back, he was gone for several years. Then he came back as an old man. He was here when he was young too. People working together, that's the way they did it. Yeah, but the Big Anderson, if we were thinking about the same one.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Same one? Why did he go to Canada? I wonder why he left?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, I guess maybe he was going to go to Canada and take a place. You know, they homesteaded Canada after they homesteaded here. But he homesteaded down here on Big Berries. Then he went to Canada, and I think he took up a homestead there too. He used to do that, you know. My brother, he did that too. My second brother, he homesteaded up here at Helmer. And then he went to Canada and took up a homestead after that. He was in that homestead business too, a long time.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Well, did, was Big Anderson really a strong man like I've heard it said?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, I suppose he wasn't a young fella, he was a big guy. I suppose he was a strong man, no doubt about that, because he was a big fella.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I've heard he was kind of an unusual fella. I mean, he wasn't, he kind of stood out as being somewhat, some different from a lot of folks. Well, I don't know. He wasn't when he was here, he was no different. Of course, I think if they teased him, he wouldn't stand for it. I heard he took a broad ax and threw it at, they were invading his cabin down here and he happened to be in it. He had a broad ax there and he'd throw it and it just missed them and hit the wall and stuck right into the wall. I don't know whether it was so or not, but that's the way they said it was anyway. But then they, you know there's some people got to tease people. You got to molest them some way. They started molesting him. He didn't think, well he wouldn't take, he wouldn't have to take anything. He'd take half a dozen of them and shook them out of their skin. Even if, he was a big guy, he was a real big man. I've heard he was dead, he was pretty religious too. Well, I think he was. I think he was a Seventh-day Advent, if I'm mistaken. I think he was supposed to be religious.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: But, I don't know, he could, he probably could be either way. Well, did you notice some people like Phil Asplen told me that he had some pretty bad sore on his leg and he walked with a cane because it didn't heal. Well, I think he had one of them ulcers. I think he did towards the last. He got an ulcer leg.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: He didn't walk with a cane when I first knew him. But then he lived to be an old man, so he probably walked with a cane. I think I heard he had an ulcer leg. You know, they get, I don't know what causes that. Varicose?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: I think so. I think it's varicose veins that causes that.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about his machine, his invention that he was working on?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, yeah. I heard about it. I didn't see it though. Perpetual motion. He was working on that, but that didn't pan out. He was working on it though.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where was it that he lived? Where was his place when he came back here that he lived?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Down there below August Anderson in the mine there. Where Arthur Anderson lived on Bear Creek. I don't know where you're across there. I've seen Bear Creek in there. I know it a little bit, but I don't know if I've been...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You know where Dan McKinsey lived?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Well, it's that old road that went by his place that crossed the August Anderson place down on the creek there. You see, McKinsey owns the August Anderson place. And where they crossed the creek, just down below there, was an old mine. I think that Big Anderson lived in that it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did he live right inside the mine shaft? Right at the head of it there?

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Yeah, he lived at the head of the shaft. In the shaft. That's what I heard. I don't know. I never was down there. But that's where he was supposed to live. That's after he'd come back from... Oh, that's quite a while after he'd come back from Canada. He got so he couldn't work. He couldn't do anything. He had trouble with that leg. And he was down in there.

speaker: Edna Johnson Butterfield or Unknown Speakers
words: Now, I'm going to...
